kanda_parva: LIX. Birth of Krishna and Baladeva
summary_en: After Devaki's earlier children are killed, the eighth birth occurs at midnight. Krishna is born, Vasudeva carries him to Yashoda, and the children are exchanged to protect him from Kansa. The episode holds both cosmic wonder and parental terror.
source_excerpt: In the auspicious midnight... Devaki gave birth... Vasudeva... repaired to the house of Yashoda... brought her daughter and placed her on Devaki's bed.

source_ref.title: A Prose English Translation of Harivamsha, Manmatha Nath Dutt

summary_en: Krishna and Balarama leave Vraja and enter Mathura, where anticipation and fear converge. Kansa prepares public spectacle and covert violence. The city becomes the stage where a private prophecy turns into political reckoning.
source_excerpt: Many... set out for Mathura... the two sons of Vasudeva entered the city while Kansa prepared the arena and his plan.

source_ref.title: A Prose English Translation of Harivamsha, Manmatha Nath Dutt

summary_en: In Kansa's arena, Krishna and Balarama face champion wrestlers Chanura and Mushtika. The contest is framed as sport but charged as execution. Krishna and Balarama win decisively, and the fear structure around Kansa begins to collapse in public view.
source_excerpt: Wrestling with Chanura... Krishna pressed his breast... Chanura... lay in the arena... Baladeva struck Mushtika... the arena grew void of wrestlers.
emotional_situations: facing intimidation in front of a crowd

source_ref.title: A Prose English Translation of Harivamsha, Manmatha Nath Dutt

summary_en: After Kansa insults Vasudeva and orders repression, Krishna leaps up, drags Kansa down, and kills him. The victory scene pivots immediately to tenderness: Krishna first bows at Vasudeva's and Devaki's feet. Justice is completed by restoration, not celebration alone.
source_excerpt: Krishna drew Kansa by the hair... dragged him in the arena... when Kansa breathed his last... Krishna first touched Vasudeva's feet and afterwards bowed at the feet of his mother.

source_ref.title: A Prose English Translation of Harivamsha, Manmatha Nath Dutt
